title: Reading and Use of English - Part 5
description: You are going to read a magazine article about a community garden project in an urban neighbourhood. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.

content.title: Growing Together
content.subtitle: A magazine article about how a community garden transformed a struggling urban neighbourhood.
content.text: The plot of land behind Greenway Avenue had been an eyesore for eleven years. It had been a car park until the building it served was demolished, a brief experiment in informal market stalls, and then, for most of a decade, simply a rectangle of cracked tarmac and accumulating rubbish at the end of a residential street in East Manchester. Nobody had owned it in any meaningful sense. Nobody had wanted to. [31] When Priya Osei first approached the council about taking it over as a community growing space, she was told, politely but firmly, that the application would join a long queue. [/31] She ignored this and started digging. || What followed is now frequently cited in urban planning circles as an example of community-led regeneration that cost almost nothing and produced results that no funded programme had managed to achieve in the same area. Within three growing seasons, the Greenway Plot — as it had come to be called — had produced food, friendships, and a measurable reduction in the low-level social tensions that had characterised the street for years. [32] None of this had been planned. It had emerged from the simple fact of people working side by side toward a shared purpose. [/32] || Priya is careful not to be sentimental about the project's early days. The initial group was small and motivation was uneven. Some neighbours joined out of genuine enthusiasm; others came because they were curious, or bored, or because Priya knocked on their door and was difficult to refuse. [33] Conflict arose early — over water use, over plot allocation, over a persistent dispute between two families about whether the correct approach to courgettes was to harvest them young or to let them grow large. [/33] These were not trivial conflicts. In a space where people were working in close proximity, minor disagreements had a way of becoming proxies for older and deeper neighbourhood tensions. || What made the Greenway Plot resilient was less any formal organisational structure than the gradual development of what sociologists call 'social capital': the accumulated trust, norms, and networks that allow groups of people to act collectively. [34] Priya describes this as 'the invisible crop' — the thing you can't weigh or sell, but without which nothing else would grow. [/34] || The plot has since become a site for activities far beyond food growing: language exchange sessions, children's workshops, an outdoor kitchen built from reclaimed materials by a group of retired builders who happened to live nearby. [35] Its success has attracted interest from the local council, which has begun to ask whether the model might be replicated elsewhere in the borough — a question Priya finds genuinely interesting and slightly alarming in equal measure. [/35] || Replication is, she thinks, the wrong framework. What worked on Greenway Avenue worked because of specific people, in a specific place, at a specific moment. [36] 'You can't copy a community,' she says. 'You can create conditions where one might grow. But only if you're patient, and only if you're prepared to step back and let it become something you didn't entirely plan.' [/36]

questions.question: What does the council's response to Priya's application suggest about the situation?
options: A) The application process for community projects was deliberately designed to discourage them.
B) The council was not interested in developing unused land in the area.
C) There were many similar requests for unused urban land being processed.
D) The council had its own plans for the land and was unwilling to discuss alternatives.
questions.explanation: The council told Priya her application would 'join a long queue', implying many similar requests existed — there was demand for unused urban land from other community groups too.

questions.question: The writer points out that the positive outcomes 'had emerged' rather than 'had been planned' in order to
options: A) argue that government investment in urban regeneration is generally wasted.
B) show that organic community activity can produce results that formal schemes cannot.
C) contrast the project's success with the failure of funded regeneration programmes.
D) suggest that social improvements cannot be engineered by external authorities.
questions.explanation: The contrast is between funded programmes that had failed and the unplanned emergence of positive outcomes through shared work — showing organic community activity achieves what formal programmes could not.

questions.question: The dispute about courgettes is mentioned to illustrate that
options: A) gardening decisions require careful management to avoid upsetting participants.
B) even small disagreements can reflect more serious underlying tensions in a community.
C) the project attracted people who had strong but conflicting opinions about horticulture.
D) practical disputes are easier to resolve than social or cultural disagreements.
questions.explanation: The writer directly explains that 'minor disagreements had a way of becoming proxies for older and deeper neighbourhood tensions', using the courgette dispute as the example that illustrates this broader pattern.

questions.question: Priya's term 'the invisible crop' refers to
options: A) the knowledge of growing techniques passed between members of the group.
B) the financial value generated by the project that is difficult to measure officially.
C) the vegetables that grow underground and cannot be seen until harvest.
D) the sense of community and mutual trust developed through shared activity.
questions.explanation: The writer explains that 'the invisible crop' is 'the accumulated trust, norms, and networks that allow groups of people to act collectively' — the social capital, not anything literally grown.

questions.question: How does Priya feel about the council's interest in replicating the project?
options: A) She is opposed to any involvement of official bodies in community projects.
B) She is enthusiastic and is actively helping to design a borough-wide programme.
C) She thinks the council does not understand what made the project successful.
D) She finds the interest both encouraging and somewhat concerning.
questions.explanation: The writer says Priya finds the council's question 'genuinely interesting and slightly alarming in equal measure' — a balanced but mixed response showing both hope and wariness.

questions.question: Priya's final comment suggests that the key to community development is
options: A) providing the right environment and allowing organic growth without over-controlling it.
B) having sufficient financial resources to support the project in its early stages.
C) ensuring that the project is documented so that others can copy the same approach.
D) finding an experienced leader who can guide the group through difficulties.
questions.explanation: Priya says you 'create conditions where one might grow' but must be 'patient' and 'prepared to step back' — emphasising that enabling an environment matters, but control and over-planning undermine it.
